filename: Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
relative_path: Guidelines/Research-related/Grants for PhD students and postdocs.docx
full_text:
´Travel grants and other grant opportunities for PhD students and postdocs

Introduction

There are a variety of grants that PhD students can apply for. Travel grant calls are often advertised via email lists and organized by the doctoral schools. Personal and salary grants are funded by private foundations, and thus are advertised on their own web pages.There are grants that you can apply for salary, for example over six months up to two years. In addition there are personal encouragement grants, especially for intermediate to late-stage PhD students, intended to be a bonus on top of your salary, or to cover costs such as the Karonkka after your defence. Some encouragement grants are awarded to earlier-stage PhD students as well, and certain grants can be given to the same person more than once (see BMH foundation), so it is a good idea to begin applying for them as soon as you fit the requirements.

How to effectively apply for travel grants

You should aim for at least one conference visit per year, and always apply for the travel grants. Please see the instructions for abstract submission.

Often the travel grants’ listed requirements include you to have a travel itinerary, an accepted poster/talk abstract etc. However, in reality the travel grant call deadlines never line up perfectly with the conference of your choice, and basically all abstracts are accepted at least as posters.

-> Thus it is important that you apply for all potential travel grants even prior to abstract submissions/acceptances. You need to also strategise - and apply the UH travel grant (where destination cannot be changed) to the conference which you at least plan to attend.

If you don't have all information the travel grant asks for, you can submit estimates of travel costs. The secretaries handling the costs associated with the travel grants are accustomed to adjusting the costs closer to the date of the conference, when the actual costs are known.

Typically the travel grants should be used within 12 months (or within the calendar year if the call deadline is in Jan-Feb)

Tip#0: Write why that conference is crucial for the development of your project.

List of travel grants

Cancer Foundation of Finland - travel grant (Deadline in May)

https://syopasaatio.fi/en/homepage/for-researchers/travel-grants/

Faculty of Medicine Travel grants twice a year (UH e-form can only be awarded max once per calendar year. Deadlines typically in January-February, and late August-September) - note that you cannot change the destination/congress

Faculty of Medicine and HiLIFE travel grants for postdocs once a year (Feb-March)

The Medicine Fund Travel Support Grants (UH e-form, Deadline typically in February)

The Magnus Ehrnrooth Foundation (for long research visits)

https://magnusehrnroothinsaatio.fi/

Tips for applying to encouragement grants

You are allowed and encouraged to apply for personal grants, but as these are extra money (in addition to your salary) the applications should not take time away from your work on projects. Thus the applications should always be done outside your work hours on your own time. Anniina is happy to provide a letter of recommendation (you provide a draft), but will not have time to comment, and Joonas can do a quick round of comments for your application.

Tip #0: Write in a project-admin-tool like Trello, or and in your calendar when are the deadlines for applying to each grant.

Tip #1: It is highly recommended that you take courses for grant writing such as those given at the University of Helsinki. In the Nature Masterclasses, which we have access till June 9th 2025 (extended longer), there is also a course about grant writing: https://masterclasses.nature.com/grant-writing/18994906

Tip#2: Prepare a super application well in advance of the deadline. Start preparing it several weeks before. Then, give it to somebody that does not know anything about your project asking for feedback. After that, improve your grant. Then store your grant for a couple of days, or weeks if possible, and then read it one last time for final improvements. Give it so much time to your application, then you can use a similar document and send it to other fundations during the year.

Tip #3: In the application, never say you are going to use the grant for karonkka costs. That will automatically ruin your chances to get any of them. After being awarded the grant, no one will know what you really use it for, so in reality you can do whatever you want with it.

The foundations awarding the encouragement grants are typically focused on one niche topic/field or another. Read the descriptions of the grants, and format your application accordingly. You should also be mindful that even though you might be doing your doctoral research leading to a PhD, the topic can still be clinically significant to warrant sending your application to e.g. the more medicine-focused foundations.

You might need to highlight a specific angle on your project for some of the applications, whereas a different aspect might be more suitable for another grant. E.g. highlighting the potential avenues for targeted therapies, or the basic biology/immunology. If the foundation asks for justification as to why you should be awarded the grant, assess what the foundation wants to support, and try to mirror that in your justification.

Tip #4: Prepare a grant with only the scopes of your first project and some perspectives. The next year you could also apply with a new project or collaboration you are performing. Usually foundations do not give grants twice to the same project

List of encouragement grants

You could maybe find more foundations in Research.fi (https://research.fi/en/)

General:

The Maud Kuistila Memorial Foundation (deadline in January)

https://mkmsaatio.fi/en/research-grants/

Idan Montinin Säätiö (deadline at the start of March)

http://www.idamontininsaatio.fi/pages/suomi/etusivu.php

Biomedicum Helsinki Foundation (deadline in the Spring, like in April)

https://biomedicum.com/participate/biomedicum-helsinki-foundation-grants/

Cancer Foundation of Finland (Deadline in the end of August)

https://syopasaatio.fi/en/homepage/

Instrumentarium (deadline early September)

https://instrufoundation.fi/foundation

Orion (Deadline early September)

https://www.orion.fi/en/rd/orion-research-foundation/

Päivikki ja Sakari Sohlbergin säätiö (Deadline in September)

https://pss-saatio.fi/

K. Albin Johansson (deadline in mid-October)
http://www.foundationweb.net/johansson/blankett.htm

The Magnus Ehrnrooth foundation (Deadline in November)

https://magnusehrnroothinsaatio.fi/en/frontpage/

Mainly for Medical students:

The Finnish Medical Foundation (Deadline in May)

https://laaketieteensaatio.fi/

Mainly for Finnish speakers or researchers working speaking in Finnish in daily basis:

Emil Aaltonen Foundation (deadline in February)

https://emilaaltonen.fi/apurahat/hae-apurahaa/

List of grants for salary

Before applying for any of these, ask Joonas/Anniina whether you should even apply for salary grants. As long as the funding situation of the lab allows for it, being salaried should always be preferred over grants (being funded by grants means many changes, including no UH benefits, MELA payments etc.).

Paulo Foundation (deadline mid-September)

https://www.paulo.fi/in-english

Yrjö Jahnsson Foundation (check the deadline)

https://www.yjs.fi/en/

Alfred Kordelin Foundation (check the deadline)

https://kordelin.fi/en/grants/for-grant-applicant/

Instrumentarium (deadline early September)

https://instrufoundation.fi/foundation

Alli Paasikivi Foundation

https://allinsaatio.fi/apurahat/tutkimusapurahat/

The Magnus Ehrnrooth Foundation

https://magnusehrnroothinsaatio.fi/

Mainly for Medical students:

The Finnish Medical Foundation (Deadline in May)

https://laaketieteensaatio.fi/
